article_meta.title: The three-way battle between Democrats running for Senate in Illinois has evolved into an argument about the best way to

transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article objectively and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words, neutral tone)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items — the 5 most objectively significant facts
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective or source type included
- Do not editorialize, adopt the article's framing, or use loaded language
- Represent all perspectives present in the article proportionally

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: The three-way battle between Democrats running for Senate in Illinois has evolved into an argument about the best way to
SOURCE: New York Times (News)

ARTICLE:

The three-way battle between Democrats running for Senate in Illinois has evolved into an argument about the best way to combat President Trump’s deportation agenda.Is it better to “abolish ICE,” as Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton offers, or to “abolish Trump’s ICE,” as Representative Raja Krishnamoorthi promises?The contest’s third candidate, Representative Robin Kelly, said the country could not just abolish U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement without a plan to replace the agency’s border enforcement function, but she introduced legislation last month to impeach Kristi Noem, the Homeland Security secretary — a bill Mr. Krishnamoorthi quickly signed onto.The tenor of the Illinois race had a focus on immigration when the Trump administration sent federal agents to Chicago last fall and then shifted fully to deportation policy after Border Patrol agents shot and killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis. The three Senate candidates, who aren’t split by major ideological or policy differences, now offer a window into how Democrats could approach dismantling Mr. Trump’s immigration enforcement structure if they win back full control in Washington.Early voting has begun for the March 17 primary race, and there is evidence in other contests that a strong anti-ICE agenda can work in low-turnout Democratic primaries. Last week in New Jersey, Analilia Mejia won a 13-candidate special primary election running as the most progressive candidate in the field on a pledge to abolish ICE. Democratic anger over Mr. Trump’s deportation policies led to a shutdown of the Department of Homeland Security when its funding expired this weekend.And yet ambitious Democrats who saw how calls to “abolish ICE” and “defund the police” hurt the party in recent years are concerned that running on a definitive message to abolish the agency is risky politics — if not in 2026 then beyond.We are having trouble retrieving the article content.Please enable JavaScript in your browser settings.Thank you for your patience while we verify access. If you are in Reader mode please exit and log into your Times account, or subscribe for all of The Times.Thank you for your patience while we verify access.Already a subscriber? Log in.Want all of The Times? Subscribe.
---

```json
{
  "summary": "Three Democrats competing in Illinois' March 17 Senate primary have centered their campaigns on immigration enforcement, debating the best approach to counter President Trump's deportation agenda. Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton advocates to \"abolish ICE,\" while Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi proposes to \"abolish Trump's ICE.\" Rep. Robin Kelly opposes abolishing ICE without a replacement plan but introduced legislation to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, which Krishnamoorthi co-signed. The race intensified after federal agents were sent to Chicago and Border Patrol agents fatally shot Alex Pretti in Minneapolis. Early voting has started, and recent evidence suggests strong anti-ICE positions can succeed in low-turnout Democratic primaries, as demonstrated by Analilia Mejia's New Jersey primary victory. However, some Democrats worry that \"abolish ICE\" messaging could pose political risks, recalling how similar slogans like \"defund the police\" previously hurt the party. Democratic opposition to Trump's deportation policies recently led to a Department of Homeland Security shutdown when funding expired.",
  "key_facts": [
    "Three Democrats—Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi, and Rep. Robin Kelly—are competing in Illinois' March 17 Senate primary with differing approaches to immigration enforcement",
    "The race's focus shifted to deportation policy after Trump sent federal agents to Chicago and Border Patrol agents killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis",
    "Analilia Mejia won a 13-candidate New Jersey special primary last week on a platform to abolish ICE, demonstrating the viability of anti-ICE messaging",
    "Rep. Robin Kelly introduced legislation to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, which Rep. Krishnamoorthi co-signed",
    "Democratic opposition to Trump's deportation policies caused a Department of Homeland Security shutdown when funding expired over the weekend"
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "New York Times reporting",
    "Campaign positions from three Illinois Senate candidates",
    "Concerns from unnamed Democratic strategists about messaging risks"
  ]
}
```

parsed_output.summary: Three Democrats competing in Illinois' March 17 Senate primary have centered their campaigns on immigration enforcement, debating the best approach to counter President Trump's deportation agenda. Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton advocates to "abolish ICE," while Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi proposes to "abolish Trump's ICE." Rep. Robin Kelly opposes abolishing ICE without a replacement plan but introduced legislation to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, which Krishnamoorthi co-signed. The race intensified after federal agents were sent to Chicago and Border Patrol agents fatally shot Alex Pretti in Minneapolis. Early voting has started, and recent evidence suggests strong anti-ICE positions can succeed in low-turnout Democratic primaries, as demonstrated by Analilia Mejia's New Jersey primary victory. However, some Democrats worry that "abolish ICE" messaging could pose political risks, recalling how similar slogans like "defund the police" previously hurt the party. Democratic opposition to Trump's deportation policies recently led to a Department of Homeland Security shutdown when funding expired.
key_facts: Three Democrats—Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi, and Rep. Robin Kelly—are competing in Illinois' March 17 Senate primary with differing approaches to immigration enforcement
The race's focus shifted to deportation policy after Trump sent federal agents to Chicago and Border Patrol agents killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis
Analilia Mejia won a 13-candidate New Jersey special primary last week on a platform to abolish ICE, demonstrating the viability of anti-ICE messaging
Rep. Robin Kelly introduced legislation to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, which Rep. Krishnamoorthi co-signed
Democratic opposition to Trump's deportation policies caused a Department of Homeland Security shutdown when funding expired over the weekend
sources_represented: Campaign positions from three Illinois Senate candidates
Concerns from unnamed Democratic strategists about messaging risks
